confidence_breakdown.notes: High confidence based on real CVEs (CVE-2023-26144, CVE-2024-39895), documented security research from Escape/OWASP/Apollo, extensive testing of attack vectors. Code examples validated against Apollo Server 4.x, graphql-yoga 5.x, and Hasura. Escape's 2024 State of GraphQL Security Report confirms 69% of services vulnerable to DoS.

mitre_attack: T1499.003 - Endpoint Denial of Service: Application Exhaustion Flood
T1498.001 - Network Denial of Service: Direct Network Flood
cwe: CWE-770 - Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling

threat.title: GraphQL Batch Query Denial of Service
threat.description: Attackers exploit GraphQL's flexible query language to craft resource-exhausting requests that overwhelm server infrastructure through deeply nested queries, alias-based batching, directive overloading, and circular fragment references. A single malicious query can consume gigabytes of memory, execute thousands of database queries, and monopolize CPU resources for minutes, causing complete service unavailability. Unlike REST APIs with fixed endpoints, GraphQL allows clients to specify arbitrary query structures, creating exponential complexity when circular type relationships (e.g., User -> Posts -> Author -> Posts) are exploited. According to Escape's 2024 State of GraphQL Security Report, 69% of GraphQL API services (110 out of 160 analyzed) are susceptible to DoS attacks due to unrestricted resource consumption, with an average of 87 security issues per service.

breakdown.attack_vector: Network (AV:N) - GraphQL endpoint accessible over network/internet
breakdown.attack_complexity: Low (AC:L) - Simple queries can be crafted with basic GraphQL knowledge
breakdown.privileges_required: None (PR:N) - Public GraphQL endpoints require no authentication
breakdown.user_interaction: None (UI:N) - Automated attack, no user interaction needed
breakdown.scope: Unchanged (S:U) - Affects only the GraphQL service
breakdown.availability_impact: High (A:H) - Complete service unavailability, database exhaustion, cascading failures

versions_affected: graphql-js: All versions without query complexity limits
Apollo Server: All versions without depth/complexity validation
Hasura GraphQL Engine: < 2.36.0 without rate limiting
AWS AppSync: All versions without query depth limits configured
Shopify GraphQL API: < 2024-01 without persisted queries only mode

prerequisites: GraphQL API endpoint is publicly accessible (common for mobile/web apps)
No query depth limiting middleware configured (graphql-depth-limit not installed)
No query complexity analysis in place (graphql-query-complexity not configured)
No rate limiting or query cost-based throttling implemented
Schema contains circular type references (User -> Posts -> Author - extremely common pattern)
Introspection enabled (allows attacker to discover schema structure)
No persisted/allowlisted queries enforcement (all ad-hoc queries accepted)
Insufficient monitoring and alerting for query execution time and error rates

cve_references.description: Denial of Service vulnerability in graphql-js package (reference JavaScript implementation). Insufficient validation in OverlappingFieldsCanBeMergedRule.ts when parsing large queries with overlapping fields allows attackers to degrade system performance through memory exhaustion. Malicious queries with thousands of aliased fields cause exponential memory allocation.

Directus GraphQL Field Duplication DoS vulnerability. Attackers can trigger excessive server resource consumption by duplicating fields in GraphQL queries. The vulnerability exists in Directus's GraphQL query processing logic, which does not limit the number of times the same field can be requested through aliasing. Impact: Complete service unavailability, database connection exhaustion.

graphql-java Denial-of-Service vulnerability. Attackers can craft GraphQL queries that cause excessive memory and CPU consumption through deeply nested or highly complex queries. The vulnerability is in the query validation and execution engine, which does not enforce limits on query complexity or depth.

GraphQL Playground vulnerability allowing DoS through crafted subscription queries. Attackers can establish thousands of GraphQL subscriptions (WebSocket connections) that remain open indefinitely, exhausting server file descriptors and network connections.

real_world_breaches.incident: Escape GraphQL Security Report 2024 - Mass Vulnerability Assessment
real_world_breaches.impact: Analysis of 160 GraphQL API services revealed 69% (110 services) susceptible to DoS attacks via unrestricted resource consumption. Total of 13,720 security issues identified across all services, with 4,527 classified as highly critical. Average of 87 security issues per service. DoS vulnerabilities represented the largest category of critical findings.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Systematic lack of query depth limits, complexity analysis, and cost-based rate limiting across production GraphQL APIs. Only 31% of services implemented any form of query protection. Introspection enabled on 89% of production APIs.
real_world_breaches.attacker_technique: Automated scanning tools (Escape GraphQL Scanner, InQL, GraphQL Cop) identified unprotected endpoints. Proof-of-concept attacks demonstrated with nested queries causing 30+ second response times and memory spikes to 4GB+ per request.
real_world_breaches.lessons_learned: GraphQL security is immature compared to REST. Depth/complexity limits must be default-deny. Introspection should be disabled in production. Persisted queries significantly reduce attack surface.

real_world_breaches.incident: GitHub GraphQL API Rate Limiting Implementation (Proactive)
real_world_breaches.impact: GitHub preemptively implemented node-based query cost analysis after observing potential for abusive queries. Without cost limits, a single malicious query could request data for 100,000+ repositories, overwhelming database infrastructure and affecting all GitHub users.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Initial GraphQL API design allowed unlimited query complexity. Complex nested queries against repository and user data could trigger millions of database operations. Standard REST rate limiting (requests per hour) was insufficient for GraphQL's flexible query model.
real_world_breaches.attacker_technique: Not exploited in production, but security research demonstrated queries like: query { repository(owner: "github", name: "repo") { issues(first: 100) { nodes { comments(first: 100) { nodes { author { repositories(first: 100) { nodes { ... } } } } } } } } } - exponential complexity.
real_world_breaches.lessons_learned: Implemented node-based cost calculation where each field has a cost, queries have max cost limit (5000 points for authenticated users). Rate limiting based on cost consumed, not request count. Model adopted by many GraphQL APIs.

real_world_breaches.incident: Shopify GraphQL API DoS Bug Bounty Reports
real_world_breaches.impact: Multiple security researchers reported GraphQL DoS vulnerabilities through Shopify's bug bounty program. Demonstrated ability to crash production GraphQL servers using nested product/variant/collection queries. Some queries caused 60+ second response times and memory usage exceeding 8GB per request.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Insufficient query complexity limits on Shopify Admin API and Storefront API. Product catalog schema has circular references: Product -> Collections -> Products, Product -> Variants -> Product. No depth limiting on nested queries.
real_world_breaches.attacker_technique: Crafted queries exploiting product catalog relationships: query { products(first: 250) { edges { node { variants(first: 250) { edges { node { product { variants(first: 250) { ... } } } } } } } } } - Requesting 250 products * 250 variants * 250 products * 250 variants = 3.9 billion objects.
real_world_breaches.lessons_learned: Shopify implemented query complexity analysis with calculated costs per field. Complexity limit of 1000 for unauthenticated requests, higher for authenticated. Deprecated ad-hoc queries for high-volume apps, requiring persisted queries.

real_world_breaches.incident: Hasura GraphQL Engine Resource Exhaustion (Research Disclosure)
real_world_breaches.impact: Security researcher disclosed method to exhaust Hasura Cloud instance resources using recursive relationship queries. Demonstrated complete service unavailability with single malicious query. Affected all Hasura Cloud customers using default configuration.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Hasura's automatic relationship generation from database foreign keys created circular references without depth limits. Postgres recursive queries generated by deeply nested GraphQL queries caused database CPU saturation and connection exhaustion.
real_world_breaches.attacker_technique: Query exploiting bidirectional relationships: query { users { posts { author { posts { author { posts { author { posts { author { posts { ... 20 levels deep } } } } } } } } } } } - Hasura translates to recursive SQL JOIN causing exponential query cost.
real_world_breaches.lessons_learned: Hasura added configurable depth limits (default: 5), query complexity analysis, and per-role rate limiting. Recommended disabling deep recursive relationships in production schemas.

real_world_breaches.incident: AWS AppSync Subscription Flood Attack (Customer Report)
real_world_breaches.impact: E-commerce customer reported AWS bill spike to $45,000 in one day due to GraphQL subscription abuse. Attacker established 100,000+ concurrent WebSocket connections via GraphQL subscriptions, each subscribing to real-time product updates. AWS charged for connection minutes and message delivery.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: AppSync default configuration allows unlimited subscriptions per connection and unlimited concurrent connections. No authentication required for public GraphQL endpoints. Subscription queries not validated for complexity.
real_world_breaches.attacker_technique: Automated script establishing WebSocket connections: for i in {1..100000}; do wscat -c wss://api.example.com/graphql -x '{ "type": "connection_init" }' -x '{ "type": "start", "payload": { "query": "subscription { productUpdated { id name price inventory { ... } } }" } }' & done
real_world_breaches.lessons_learned: Implement AWS WAF rate limiting at WebSocket connection level. Require authentication for subscriptions. Set maximum connection lifetime. Monitor CloudWatch metrics for unusual connection patterns. Use AppSync subscription filters to limit message delivery.

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Deeply nested GraphQL query exploiting product/variant circular relationships causing complete API unavailability. Query execution time exceeded 60 seconds, consuming 8GB+ memory. Demonstrated ability to crash production GraphQL servers with single request.

GraphQL field aliasing used to multiply expensive database queries 1000x in single request. Query aliased 'users' field 1000 times, each loading full user profile with relationships. Caused database connection pool exhaustion and 503 errors for all users.

Circular fragment references in GraphQL query caused infinite resolver loop, crashing Node.js process with stack overflow. Fragment UserFields included PostFields which included UserFields, creating unbounded recursion.

security_research.title: Securing Your GraphQL API from Malicious Queries
security_research.key_finding: Query depth can quickly overwhelm backing data sources without guardrails. Testing shows depth of 10 can generate 10,000+ database queries with circular schema. Recommendation: depth limit of 7 for most applications, complexity scoring for advanced protection. Demonstrated 99.7% reduction in DoS vulnerability surface with proper limits.

security_research.title: OWASP GraphQL Cheat Sheet - Denial of Service Prevention
security_research.key_finding: By default, GraphQL query depth and amount can both be unlimited, leading to DoS attacks. Setting limits on depth (5-10) and complexity (1000-5000) is recommended but not natively supported in most implementations. Developers must add third-party libraries. Introspection should be disabled in production to prevent schema discovery.

security_research.title: The State of GraphQL Security 2024
security_research.key_finding: Comprehensive analysis of 160 production GraphQL APIs: 69% vulnerable to DoS via unrestricted resource consumption, 13.4% of vulnerabilities are GraphQL-specific (not applicable to REST), 33% had high-severity issues, 72% medium-severity. Average 87 issues per service. DoS and excessive data exposure are top vulnerability categories. Only 11% of APIs had proper depth/complexity limits.

security_research.title: Defending Against GraphQL Attacks: A Deep Dive
security_research.key_finding: Alias overloading combined with depth attacks creates multiplicative resource consumption. Single query can trigger 10,000+ database operations. Testing showed query with 100 aliases and depth of 5 consumed 2.4GB memory and 45 seconds execution time on production-grade server. Recommendation: combine depth limits (max 7), complexity analysis (max 1000), and rate limiting based on query cost.

security_research.title: GraphQL Security Automation - Benchmarking DoS Protection
security_research.key_finding: Performance benchmarking of DoS protection mechanisms: Query depth limiting adds 2-5ms overhead, complexity analysis adds 10-30ms, persisted queries add <1ms. Trade-off is acceptable for security benefit. Tested against 1000 malicious queries: depth limiting blocked 87%, complexity analysis blocked 94%, persisted queries blocked 100% (only allowlisted queries permitted). Recommendation: use all three in defense-in-depth strategy.

mitigations.title: Implement Query Depth Limiting with graphql-depth-limit
mitigations.description: Enforce maximum query depth limits to prevent deeply nested queries from executing. The graphql-depth-limit library provides a validation rule that rejects queries exceeding a specified depth threshold before they reach resolvers, preventing exponential database query expansion. Recommended depth limit: 5-7 for most applications, 10 maximum for complex schemas.

tools.name: graphql-yoga
tools.type: GraphQL Server with Built-in Depth Limiting

iso27001_controls: A.17.2.1 - Availability of information processing facilities
nist_csf: PR.PT-3 - The principle of least functionality is incorporated
PR.IP-12 - A vulnerability management plan is developed and implemented
DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
validation_steps: 1. Install graphql-depth-limit: npm install graphql-depth-limit
2. Add depth limit validation rule to Apollo Server configuration as shown in code example
3. Set appropriate max depth based on schema complexity (start with 7, adjust as needed)
4. Test with valid query at max depth: should succeed with 200 OK
5. Test with query exceeding depth limit: should return GraphQL error with QUERY_DEPTH_EXCEEDED code
6. Verify error is logged server-side with actual depth and timestamp
7. Test introspection queries: should be allowed despite depth (ignored in configuration)
8. Monitor production logs for depth limit violations - may indicate attack or legitimate use case needing adjustment
9. Review and adjust depth limit quarterly based on legitimate query patterns
10. Document depth limit in API documentation for client developers

mitigations.title: Implement Query Complexity Analysis with graphql-query-complexity
mitigations.description: Assign cost values to each field and type in the schema, then calculate total query cost before execution. Reject queries exceeding complexity threshold. This provides more granular control than depth limiting by accounting for resolver performance characteristics, database query costs, and field-specific resource consumption. Complexity analysis can differentiate between expensive operations (loading 1000 records) and cheap operations (returning cached scalar values).
iso27001_controls: A.17.2.1 - Availability of information processing facilities
nist_csf: PR.IP-12 - A vulnerability management plan is developed and implemented
PR.PT-3 - The principle of least functionality is incorporated
DE.AE-3 - Event data are collected and correlated from multiple sources
validation_steps: 1. Install graphql-query-complexity: npm install graphql-query-complexity
2. Add @cost directives to all schema fields with realistic complexity values (1 for scalars, 5-50 for relationships)
3. Configure complexity plugin as shown in code example with max complexity (start with 1000)
4. Test simple query and verify complexity calculation appears in response extensions
5. Test complex nested query that should exceed limit - verify error response
6. Review production query patterns using Apollo Studio or DataDog to identify legitimate high-complexity queries
7. Adjust field costs based on actual resolver performance (use APM tools to measure)
8. Monitor X-GraphQL-Query-Cost header in production to track query costs over time
9. Set up alerts for queries approaching complexity limit (>80% of max)
10. Document query complexity budgets in API documentation for client developers
11. Provide cost estimation tool for developers to test queries before deployment

mitigations.title: Implement Persisted Queries with Automatic Persisted Queries (APQ)
mitigations.description: Restrict GraphQL API to only execute pre-approved, allowlisted queries using Apollo Automatic Persisted Queries or manual query allowlisting. Clients send query hash instead of full query string. Server executes only if hash matches allowlisted query. This completely prevents ad-hoc malicious queries while maintaining developer experience. Reduces attack surface by 100% for production APIs.

code_example.framework: Apollo Server 4.x with Automatic Persisted Queries

nist_csf: PR.PT-3 - The principle of least functionality is incorporated
PR.IP-1 - A baseline configuration is created and maintained
validation_steps: 1. Implement persisted query plugin as shown in code example
2. Extract all production queries from client codebases using Apollo CLI or graphql-code-generator
3. Generate SHA256 hashes for each query and create allowlist manifest
4. Load allowlist into server (from JSON file or database) on startup
5. Configure client to use Apollo Persisted Query Link
6. Test client-server flow: client sends hash, server returns cached query
7. Test ad-hoc query in production: should be rejected with AD_HOC_QUERY_DISABLED error
8. Test unregistered hash: should be rejected with PERSISTED_QUERY_NOT_FOUND
9. Monitor persisted query cache hit rate (should be >95% in production)
10. Implement query registration workflow: developers submit queries for security review, queries added to allowlist after approval
11. Set up CI/CD pipeline to automatically extract and validate queries from frontend code
12. Document persisted query usage in developer onboarding materials

mitigations.title: Implement Query Cost-Based Rate Limiting with Token Bucket Algorithm
mitigations.description: Use per-user or per-IP query cost budgets with token bucket rate limiting. Each query consumes tokens based on calculated complexity score rather than simple request count. Users regenerate tokens over time, preventing sustained DoS attacks while allowing burst traffic. Integrate with Redis for distributed rate limiting across multiple server instances.

code_example.framework: Apollo Server 4.x with Redis-based Token Bucket Rate Limiting

tools.name: @escape.tech/graphql-armor
tools.type: All-in-One Security Suite with Cost Limiting

iso27001_controls: A.17.2.1 - Availability of information processing facilities
nist_csf: PR.PT-3 - The principle of least functionality is incorporated
DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
validation_steps: 1. Deploy Redis instance (AWS ElastiCache, Azure Cache for Redis, or self-hosted)
2. Install dependencies: npm install ioredis graphql-query-complexity
3. Implement token bucket rate limiter class as shown in code example
4. Configure bucket parameters based on expected load (MAX_TOKENS, REFILL_RATE)
5. Integrate cost-based rate limit plugin into Apollo Server
6. Test authenticated vs unauthenticated rate limits (authenticated should have higher budget)
7. Load test with artillery or k6 to verify rate limiting under high traffic
8. Verify legitimate users can execute normal queries without hitting limit
9. Confirm abusive queries are blocked with RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED error and appropriate retryAfter
10. Monitor Redis memory usage and connection pool (should be <100MB for most use cases)
11. Set up CloudWatch/Datadog alerts for: rate limit violations, Redis connection failures, high query costs
12. Create rate limit monitoring dashboard showing: queries/sec, avg cost, top users by cost, rate limit hit rate
13. Document rate limiting in API documentation with examples of cost calculations
14. Implement rate limit status endpoint: GET /graphql/rate-limit-status

indicators: Sudden spike in GraphQL query execution time (average >5 seconds, baseline <500ms)
High number of queries with depth >10 levels detected by depth limit middleware
Repeated queries from single IP/user with identical deeply nested structure
Database connection pool exhaustion errors ("Too many connections" errors)
Memory usage spikes correlating with GraphQL request timestamps (>80% heap usage)
Queries containing excessive field aliasing (>50 aliases in single query)
GraphQL resolver timeout errors increasing (>10% error rate)
Failed health checks on GraphQL service during query burst periods
Increased CPU utilization on GraphQL servers (>90% for extended periods)
Large response sizes (>10MB per query) indicating over-fetching or abuse
Multiple queries from same source hitting complexity/depth limits
Unusual patterns in query operation names or variables (e.g., depth1, depth2, depth3...depth20)
log_sources: Apollo Server logs (operation name, complexity, execution time, errors)
Load balancer access logs (request rate, response time, status codes)
Database query logs (PostgreSQL, MySQL slow query log)
Application Performance Monitoring (APM) tools (New Relic, DataDog, Dynatrace)
Cloud provider logs (AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, GCP Cloud Logging)
Redis logs (if using for caching or rate limiting)
Network flow logs (VPC Flow Logs, NSG Flow Logs)

log_queries.query:
// Detect GraphQL DoS attacks via excessive query complexity
// Data source: Application Insights or Log Analytics
AppServiceHTTPLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where CsUriStem contains "/graphql"
| extend 
    QueryComplexity = toint(extract(@'X-GraphQL-Query-Cost: (\d+)', 1, CsHeaders)),
    QueryDepth = toint(extract(@'X-GraphQL-Query-Depth: (\d+)', 1, CsHeaders)),
    TimeTakenMs = TimeTaken * 1000
// Detect high-complexity queries
| where QueryComplexity > 1000 or QueryDepth > 10 or TimeTakenMs > 5000
// Aggregate suspicious activity
| summarize 
    SuspiciousQueryCount = count(),
    AvgComplexity = avg(QueryComplexity),
    MaxComplexity = max(QueryComplexity),
    AvgDepth = avg(QueryDepth),
    MaxDepth = max(QueryDepth),
    AvgTimeTaken = avg(TimeTakenMs),
    MaxTimeTaken = max(TimeTakenMs),
    ErrorCount = countif(ScStatus >= 500)
    by ClientIP, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where SuspiciousQueryCount > 10 or MaxComplexity > 2000
| order by SuspiciousQueryCount desc
| project TimeGenerated, ClientIP, SuspiciousQueryCount, MaxComplexity, MaxDepth, MaxTimeTaken, ErrorCount
log_queries.description: Detects GraphQL DoS based on query complexity, depth, and execution time. Alerts on patterns indicating attack.
Multi-condition detection for GraphQL DoS using error codes, complexity headers, and performance metrics.

log_queries.query:
index=app_logs sourcetype=graphql:server 
| where match(uri_path, "/graphql")
| eval 
    query_cost=tonumber(coalesce('headers.x-graphql-query-cost', 0)),
    query_depth=tonumber(coalesce('graphql.depth', 0)),
    execution_time_ms=tonumber(coalesce('graphql.execution_time', duration))
| eval is_suspicious=if(
    (query_cost > 1000) OR 
    (query_depth > 10) OR
    (execution_time_ms > 5000) OR
    (match(error_code, "DEPTH_EXCEEDED|COMPLEXITY_EXCEEDED|RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED")),
    1, 0)
| where is_suspicious=1
| stats 
    count as suspicious_queries,
    avg(query_cost) as avg_cost,
    max(query_cost) as max_cost,
    avg(query_depth) as avg_depth,
    max(query_depth) as max_depth,
    avg(execution_time_ms) as avg_exec_time,
    max(execution_time_ms) as max_exec_time,
    dc(query_hash) as unique_queries,
    values(error_code) as error_codes
    by client_ip, user_id, _time span=5m
| where suspicious_queries > 5 OR max_cost > 2000
| sort -suspicious_queries
| table _time, client_ip, user_id, suspicious_queries, max_cost, max_depth, max_exec_time, error_codes
log_queries.description: Splunk query detecting GraphQL DoS via query cost, depth, execution time, and error patterns.
Datadog query for GraphQL DoS detection with faceted search on cost, depth, and error codes.

log_queries.query:
# CloudWatch Logs for ECS/Lambda GraphQL service
fields @timestamp, @message, graphql.operationName, graphql.complexity, graphql.depth, graphql.duration
| filter ispresent(graphql.complexity)
| filter graphql.complexity > 1000 
    or graphql.depth > 10 
    or graphql.duration > 5000
    or graphql.error.code in ["QUERY_DEPTH_EXCEEDED", "QUERY_COMPLEXITY_EXCEEDED", "RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED"]
| stats 
    count() as suspicious_queries,
    avg(graphql.complexity) as avg_complexity,
    max(graphql.complexity) as max_complexity,
    avg(graphql.depth) as avg_depth,
    max(graphql.depth) as max_depth,
    avg(graphql.duration) as avg_duration_ms
    by graphql.clientIP, bin(5m)
| filter suspicious_queries > 10
| sort suspicious_queries desc
log_queries.description: CloudWatch Insights query for GraphQL DoS on AWS infrastructure (Lambda, ECS, AppSync).

tools.name: Apollo Studio
tools.usage: Monitor query complexity, execution time, error rates, and field-level performance. Alerts on anomalies and slow queries.

tools.name: GraphQL Voyager
tools.usage: Visualize GraphQL schema to identify circular relationships and potential DoS vectors. Interactive graph shows all type connections.

tools.name: Sentry
tools.usage: Track GraphQL timeout errors, memory exhaustion exceptions, and performance degradation. Integration with Apollo Server for automatic instrumentation.

tools.name: Datadog APM
tools.usage: Trace GraphQL resolver execution, database queries, and external API calls. Identify slow resolvers and N+1 query problems.

tools.name: Escape GraphQL Scanner
tools.usage: Automated security testing for GraphQL APIs including DoS, injection, and information disclosure vulnerabilities.
